aiashley — global digest Generated 16:35 UTC

Tuesday, March 17, 2026

International News · 16 Sources · 4 Regions

Daily Brief

The US-Israeli war against Iran entered its 18th day with significant developments on multiple fronts. Israel claimed to have killed Iran's security chief Ali Larijani and Basij commander Gholamreza Soleimani in overnight airstrikes [BBC, Globe and Mail, Le Monde], potentially the most significant targeting of Iranian leadership since Supreme Leader Khamenei was killed on February 28. In a notable break from the administration, National Counterterrorism Center Director Joe Kent resigned, stating Iran posed 'no imminent threat' and accusing Israeli lobbying of pushing the US into war [BBC, Le Monde, Globe and Mail]. President Trump's calls for NATO allies to help secure the Strait of Hormuz were rebuffed by European capitals, with Germany's defense minister asking what 'a handful of European frigates' could accomplish that the US Navy cannot [Le Monde, BBC]. The economic ripple effects are spreading globally: Sri Lanka announced a four-day work week to conserve fuel [ABC Australia], while British holidaymakers are rebooking Easter trips away from Dubai [BBC]. Separately, Pakistan conducted an airstrike on a Kabul drug rehabilitation center killing over 100 people [BBC], and a Brussels court ordered 93-year-old former Belgian diplomat Étienne Davignon to stand trial over the 1961 assassination of Congolese leader Patrice Lumumba [Guardian, RFI, France 24].

Top Stories
01 Conflict

Israel Claims Killing of Iran's Security Chief Larijani in Escalating Air Campaign

  • Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz announced that Ali Larijani, secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council, and Basij paramilitary commander Gholamreza Soleimani were killed in overnight airstrikes near Tehran
  • Larijani had become the de facto leader of Iran's war efforts since Supreme Leader Khamenei was killed on February 28; his death would be the highest-profile assassination since then
  • Iran has not confirmed either death; both officials were central to Iran's violent crackdown on protests in January challenging the theocracy's rule
  • Prime Minister Netanyahu's office released a photo of him ordering the eliminations, while the IDF described Larijani as 'one of the most veteran and senior figures within the Iranian regime'
Coverage
BBC WorldDetailed military and strategic context, emphasized Larijani's role as de facto leader Le MondeFocused on Israeli government messaging and Netanyahu's involvement Globe and MailEmphasized broader war context including Gulf attacks and energy crisis concerns ABC AustraliaProvided biographical background on Larijani as pragmatic diplomat
02 Policy

Top US Counterterrorism Official Resigns, Says Iran War Driven by Israeli Lobby Pressure

  • Joe Kent, director of the National Counterterrorism Center, resigned stating he 'cannot in good conscience support the ongoing war in Iran'
  • Kent's resignation letter accused the administration of starting the war 'due to pressure from Israel and its powerful American lobby,' claiming Iran posed 'no imminent threat'
  • Kent is a former Green Beret with 11 combat deployments; he is the first senior Trump administration official to resign over the Iran war
  • The White House dismissed the letter as containing 'many false claims'; Trump called Kent 'weak on security' and said the resignation showed 'it was a good thing that he's out'
Coverage
BBC WorldLed with White House response and Trump's dismissive comments Le MondeEmphasized Kent's military credentials and quoted extensively from resignation letter Globe and MailNoted Kent's past ties to right-wing extremists and controversial confirmation process The HinduIncluded background on Kent's previous congressional campaigns and far-right associations
03 Geopolitics

European Allies Refuse Trump's Demand to Join Strait of Hormuz Operation

  • Trump called on NATO allies plus Japan, South Korea, and China to send warships to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, threatening it would be 'very bad for the future of NATO' if they refuse
  • EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas stated 'This is not Europe's war,' while Germany's defense minister asked what European frigates could do that the US Navy cannot
  • France confirmed it will not send naval vessels, maintaining a 'strictly defensive approach' and refusing to join the war on Iran
  • Trump later reversed course, posting that the US 'no longer need, or desire' allied assistance, adding 'WE DO NOT NEED THE HELP OF ANYONE'
Coverage
Le MondeDetailed European diplomatic responses, emphasized France's refusal BBC EuropeIncluded criticism from former UK defense chief that NATO wasn't designed for 'wars of choice' The HinduLed with Trump's reversal claiming US doesn't need help RFIFocused specifically on French position and defensive posture
04 Conflict

Pakistan Airstrike Kills Over 100 at Kabul Drug Rehabilitation Center

  • Pakistani air strikes killed more than 100 people at the Omid Addiction Treatment Hospital in Kabul, according to forensic laboratory sources
  • The facility was treating approximately 2,000 patients; many bodies were reportedly injured beyond recognition
  • Pakistan denied deliberately targeting the hospital, claiming it struck 'military installations and terrorist support infrastructure'
  • The UN Assistance Mission in Afghanistan condemned the strike and called for an immediate ceasefire and investigation; Afghan health ministry said no military facilities were near the center
Coverage
BBC WorldDetailed casualty figures and UN response, included Pakistan's denial France 24Video coverage noting Pakistan's claim of targeting 'terrorist' targets
05 Society

Former Belgian Diplomat Ordered to Stand Trial Over 1961 Assassination of Patrice Lumumba

  • Brussels court ordered 93-year-old Étienne Davignon, former EU Commission vice-president, to stand trial for alleged war crimes related to the killing of Congo's first prime minister
  • Davignon is charged with participation in unlawful detention and transfer of Lumumba and denying him a fair trial; a charge of intent to kill was dismissed
  • Lumumba was tortured and executed by firing squad in January 1961 by Belgian-backed separatists; his body was dissolved in acid
  • Lumumba's grandson Mehdi Lumumba called the decision 'historic,' saying 'Belgium is finally confronting its history'; Davignon denies all charges
Coverage
The GuardianEmphasized historical context and Belgian parliamentary finding of 'moral responsibility' RFIFramed as landmark moment in Europe's colonial reckoning France 24Brief coverage noting this is first prosecution in the case
Also Today
  • Sri Lanka introduces four-day work week to conserve fuel amid Middle East war and Strait of Hormuz closure ABC Australia
  • UK health minister describes meningitis outbreak linked to Canterbury nightclub as 'unprecedented' after two student deaths and 15 confirmed cases Le Monde
  • At least 23 killed in suspected Boko Haram suicide bombings in Maiduguri, Nigeria during Ramadan iftar The Guardian
  • British holidaymakers rebooking Easter trips from Dubai to Spain and Portugal amid Middle East war disruption BBC Europe
  • Former French president Nicolas Sarkozy returns to court for appeal trial over alleged Libyan campaign financing RFI
  • UK and Ukraine announce partnership to sell drone technology abroad as Zelensky visits London Globe and Mail
  • OpenAI signs deal to provide AI models to Pentagon for classified work through Amazon Web Services after Anthropic dropped Globe and Mail
  • Chile's new president José Antonio Kast begins construction of border barrier with Peru five days after taking office BBC World
  • French national Martin Ryan sentenced to 10 years in Azerbaijan prison for spying on military cooperation with Turkey and Pakistan BBC Europe
  • Cuba experiences third blackout in four months as Trump escalates threats and claims he could 'take Cuba' Globe and Mail
